# Improving COMP110 Through Low-Stakes Programming Practice

## Project Overview

This website presents my EX09 data analysis project for COMP110. The goal of the project was to use anonymized course survey data to identify a possible way to improve the course. My analysis focuses on whether COMP110 should add more low-stakes, quiz-style programming practice for students, especially students with less prior programming experience.

I chose this idea because programming confidence and course difficulty are major parts of the COMP110 experience. If students can practice more often in lower-pressure settings, they may feel more prepared for quizzes, assignments, and larger programming tasks.

## My Course Improvement Idea

My recommendation is that COMP110 should add more low-stakes quiz-style programming practice. This could include short practice problems, optional review quizzes, small coding warm-ups, or guided practice sets before graded quizzes and programming assignments.

The main stakeholder group is students enrolled in COMP110. This idea could create value by helping students build confidence, check their understanding earlier, and feel less overwhelmed by the pace and difficulty of the course.

## Data Used

For this project, I used anonymized COMP110 course survey data. I focused on survey columns related to:

- prior programming experience
- perceived course difficulty
- understanding of course material
- programming assignment effectiveness
- quiz effectiveness
- whether students would recommend the course

These variables helped me explore whether students’ experiences with programming practice and quizzes are related to their overall understanding and view of the course.

## Visualization 1: Prior Programming Experience

<img src="/improved_course/static/imgs/prior_exp_plot.png" alt="Chart showing students' prior programming experience" width="700"/>

This visualization shows the distribution of students’ prior programming experience before taking COMP110. Many students entered the course with little or no prior programming experience. This matters because students with less experience may need more frequent opportunities to practice before high-stakes assessments.

## Visualization 2: Prior Experience and Course Difficulty

<img src="/improved_course/static/imgs/difficulty_plot.png" alt="Chart comparing prior programming experience and course difficulty" width="700"/>

This visualization compares students’ prior programming experience with their reported difficulty in the course. The goal of this chart is to see whether students with less programming background tend to experience COMP110 as more difficult.

## Visualization 3: Programming Practice and Understanding

<img src="/improved_course/static/imgs/programming_plot.png" alt="Chart comparing programming assignment effectiveness and understanding" width="700"/>

This visualization explores whether students who found programming assignments effective also reported stronger understanding of the course material. This helps support the idea that practice-based learning is important in COMP110.

## Visualization 4: Quiz Effectiveness and Course Recommendation

<img src="/improved_course/static/imgs/quiz_plot.png" alt="Chart comparing quiz effectiveness and course recommendation" width="700"/>

This visualization looks at the relationship between quiz effectiveness and whether students would recommend the course. If students who found quizzes more effective also rated the course more positively, that supports the idea that better quiz preparation and practice could improve the student experience.

## Conclusion

Based on my analysis, the data supports the idea that COMP110 could benefit from more low-stakes programming practice. Students come into the course with different levels of prior experience, and programming practice appears connected to understanding and overall course satisfaction.

My recommendation is not to make the course easier or remove challenging material. Instead, COMP110 should give students more chances to practice before major graded work. This could help students identify confusion earlier, reduce stress, and build stronger programming habits.

One possible downside is that adding more practice could create extra work for students and instructors. To avoid this, the practice should be short, optional or low-stakes, and focused on the most important skills for upcoming assignments and quizzes.

In the future, COMP110 could collect more specific data about which types of practice students use most often and which practice activities are most helpful. That would make it easier to decide exactly what kind of low-stakes programming practice should be added.
